---
title: "2.9 Implementing Selection and Iteration Algorithms"
subtitle: "Part 2: Min, Max, Divisibility, and Prime"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit2-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Loop with if

### Minimum and maximum

A common variation of the accumulator pattern is to have an `if` statement inside the loop that tests each value being considered in the loop.

This is a very common pattern in the AP exam for FRQ #1.

To determine the minimum or maximum value in a sequence of numbers, use a variable to store the current minimum or maximum value.

## Minimum Example

### Update when a lower value is found

Use a loop to go through the sequence of numbers and update the minimum or maximum value if you find a number that is lower or higher than the current minimum or maximum.

```java
int min = Integer.MAX_VALUE;
for (int i = 0; i < 10; i++)
{
    int num = (int)(Math.random() * 100);
    System.out.println(num);
    if (num < min)
    {
        min = num;
    }
}
System.out.println("The minimum value is " + min);
```

## Code Task

### `activecode:: min-max-practice`

Textbook prompt: Run to see the `min` method in action.

Then, complete the `max` method below to find the maximum of a set of random numbers.

## Code Window

### `activecode:: min-max-practice`

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class MinMax
{
    // min calculates and returns the minimum of n random numbers
    public static int min(int n)
    {
        int min = Integer.MAX_VALUE;
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
        {
            int num = (int)(Math.random() * 100);
            System.out.println(num);
            if (num < min)
            {
                min = num;
            }
        }

        return min;
    }

    // max calculates and returns the maximum of n random numbers
    public static int max(int n)
    {
        int max = Integer.MIN_VALUE;
        // TODO: write a loop to find the maximum value
        // of n random numbers from 0 to 100


        return max;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        int n = 10;
        System.out.println("The minimum value is " + min(n));
        System.out.println("The maximum value is " + max(n));
    }
}
```
:::

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: min-max-practice`

Runestone checks that:

::: {.tight-list}
- each method has a `for` loop that uses `n`
- the submitted code contains `num > max`
:::

## Divisibility

### Remainder operator

To determine if an integer is evenly divisible by another integer, you can use the remainder operator `%`.

If you divide two numbers and there is no remainder, then the first number is evenly divisible by the second number.

The remainder operator is often used to check if a number is even or odd: if `num % 2 == 0` is true, then `num` is even.

## Divisibility Example

### No remainder means evenly divisible

```java
int num1 = 10;
int num2 = 5;
if (num1 % num2 == 0)
{
    System.out.println(num1 + " is evenly divisible by " + num2);
}
else
{
    System.out.println(num1 + " is not evenly divisible by " + num2);
}
```

## Divisibility and Prime

### Put the selection statement in a loop

The selection statement above can be put in a loop to determine if a number is divisible by a range of numbers.

For example, you can determine if a number is prime by checking if it is divisible by any number between `2` and `n-1`, or even the square root of the number since multiples of numbers repeat after the square root.

Remember that a <span class="term">prime number</span> is a number that is only divisible by `1` and itself, so if you find any other divisor, the number is not prime.

## Code Task

### `activecode:: isPrimeLoop`

Textbook prompt: Complete the `isPrime` method below to determine if a number `n` is prime by checking if it is evenly divisible by any number between `2` and `n-1` or the square root of `n`.

## Code Window

### `activecode:: isPrimeLoop`

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class PrimeNumbers
{
    // isPrime checks whether n is a prime number
    public static boolean isPrime(int n)
    {
        // Add a loop from 2 to n-1 or Math.sqrt(n)
        //   if n is divisible by the loop variable i
        //        return false



        // if no divisor found after loop, return true
        return true;
    }


    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        System.out.println("Is 4 prime? " + isPrime(4));
        System.out.println("Is 5 prime? " + isPrime(5));
    }
}
```
:::

## Test Requirements

### `activecode:: isPrimeLoop`

Runestone checks that:

::: {.tight-list}
- the code contains a `for` loop
- `main` prints `Is 4 prime? false` and `Is 5 prime? true`
- `isPrime(9)` returns `false`
- the code contains the divisor test `n % i == 0`
:::

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- explain why min starts with `Integer.MAX_VALUE`
- explain why max starts with `Integer.MIN_VALUE`
- update min or max only inside an `if` when a better value is found
- use `%` to test whether one integer is evenly divisible by another
- describe prime checking as searching for any divisor other than `1` and itself
- return `false` as soon as a divisor is found
:::

## End

### Continue to Part 3

[Next: Digits, random, and frequency →](2-9-part-3-digits-random-and-frequency.html)
